stimulus: Petra Andersen, Robert Kaplan, and Ingrid Lund have hypothesized that the long, paddle-shaped tail of the marine reptile Mosasaurus hoffmannii primarily allowed the animal to accelerate rapidly when launching ambush attacks on fast-moving prey near the surface. To evaluate their hypothesis, a separate research team built two battery-powered mechanical models of M. hoffmannii—one with the paddle-shaped tail and one with a smooth, tapered eel-like tail of equal mass—and subjected both models to a series of identical trials in a large saltwater testing tank.
stem: Which finding from the model trials, if true, would most strongly support Andersen and colleagues' hypothesis?

answerOptions.content: The model with the paddle-shaped tail reached a target burst speed in significantly less time, starting from rest, than the model with the tapered tail did.
The model with the paddle-shaped tail displaced significantly more water when fully submerged than the model with the tapered tail did.
The model with the paddle-shaped tail used significantly more battery power across the full series of trials than the model with the tapered tail did.
The model with the paddle-shaped tail took significantly longer to swim a steady fixed distance at constant speed than the model with the tapered tail did.

rationale:
Choice A is the best answer because it most directly supports Andersen and colleagues' hypothesis that the paddle-shaped tail of M. hoffmannii primarily allowed for rapid acceleration in ambush attacks. Reaching a target burst speed in significantly less time starting from rest is precisely a measure of rapid acceleration; that the paddle-tailed model outperforms the tapered model on this measure directly supports the hypothesized function.

Choice B is incorrect because water displacement reflects the model's volume, not its acceleration; it does not test the proposed function of the paddle tail. Choice C is incorrect because greater battery use says only that the paddle tail is energetically more demanding, not that it provides faster acceleration. Choice D is incorrect because slower steady-state swimming over a fixed distance would, if anything, suggest the paddle tail is worse at sustained cruising; the hypothesis is about acceleration in ambush, not about sustained-speed travel, so this finding does not support the hypothesis (and could even weaken claims about general locomotor superiority).
